content:
# Genesis Chapter 48 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28r–28v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_048_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_048_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 48–49 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0052

---

## The News Reaches Joseph

**1** And it came about after these things that one said to Joseph, "Behold, your father is ill." So he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim.

**2** And it was told to Jacob: "Behold, your son Joseph has come to you." So Israel summoned his strength and sat up on the bed.

---

## Jacob Recalls the Promise of El Shaddai

**3** And Jacob said to Joseph, "El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and he blessed me,

**4** and said to me, 'Behold, I will make you fruitful and will multiply you, and I will make you into a company of peoples, and I will give this land to your offspring after you as an everlasting possession.'

---

## The Adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh

**5** "And now, your two sons who were born to you in the land of Egypt before I came to you in Egypt — they are mine; Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine, as Reuben and Simeon are mine.

**6** But the children whom you father after them shall be yours; they shall be called by the name of their brothers in their inheritance.

---

## The Death of Rachel Remembered

**7** "As for me, when I came from Paddan, Rachel died beside me in the land of Canaan on the way, when there was still some distance to go to Ephrath; and I buried her there on the way to Ephrath" — that is, Bethlehem.

---

## The Crossing of Hands

**8** Then Israel saw Joseph's sons and said, "Who are these?"

**9** And Joseph said to his father, "They are my sons, whom God has given me here." And he said, "Bring them to me, please, that I may bless them."

**10** Now the eyes of Israel were heavy with age, so that he could not see. And Joseph brought them near to him, and he kissed them and embraced them.

**11** And Israel said to Joseph, "I never expected to see your face, and behold, God has let me see your offspring also."

**12** Then Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth.

**13** And Joseph took the two of them — Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right — and brought them near to him.

**14** But Israel stretched out his right hand and laid it on the head of Ephraim, who was the younger, and his left hand on the head of Manasseh — he crossed his hands deliberately, for Manasseh was the firstborn.

---

## The Threefold Blessing

**15** And he blessed Joseph and said:

"The God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked,
the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this day,

**16** the angel who has redeemed me from all evil —
may he bless the lads;
and in them let my name be carried on,
and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac;
and let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth."

---

## Joseph's Protest and Jacob's Insistence

**17** When Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand on the head of Ephraim, it was wrong in his eyes; and he took hold of his father's hand to remove it from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's head.

**18** And Joseph said to his father, "Not so, my father, for this one is the firstborn; put your right hand on his head."

**19** But his father refused and said, "I know, my son, I know. He also shall become a people, and he also shall be great. Nevertheless his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his offspring shall become a fullness of nations."

**20** So he blessed them that day, saying:

"By you Israel will pronounce blessings, saying,
'May God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh.'"

And so he set Ephraim before Manasseh.

---

## Final Words to Joseph

**21** Then Israel said to Joseph, "Behold, I am about to die, but God will be with you and will bring you back to the land of your fathers.

**22** Moreover, I have given to you rather than to your brothers one portion, which I took from the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow."

---

## Translation Notes

**Note 1 — v. 2: "summoned his strength" (wayyitchazzeq Yisra'el)**
The verb chazaq (hithpael: "to strengthen oneself, to exert oneself") describes Jacob's deliberate, effortful act of rallying his fading body. The same root is used in Isa 35:4 ("strengthen the feeble hands"). The name "Israel" is used here, not "Jacob" — the dying patriarch is acting in his covenant-representative capacity. The formal "sat up on the bed" (wayyeshev al-ha-mittah) contrasts with the frailty implied by the very effort required. The whole verse captures the tension between physical decay and the sovereign authority still resident in the dying patriarch.

**Note 2 — v. 3: El Shaddai — the name and the site**
"El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz" recalls Gen 35:9-15, where God appeared to Jacob at Bethel (= Luz, the older name; cf. 28:19) and confirmed the patriarchal covenant under the name El Shaddai. The same theophany is recalled in 35:11 with the identical language ("I am El Shaddai; be fruitful and multiply"). Jacob's retrospective invocation of this encounter here — on his deathbed — gives the adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh its theological warrant: the blessing Jacob now confers is not personal sentiment but a transmission of the covenant promise vouchsafed by El Shaddai himself. The name is preserved untranslated per project convention.

**Note 3 — v. 4: "everlasting possession" (achuzzat olam)**
The noun achuzzah means "holding, possession, property" (from the root achaz, "to take hold of"). It appears in the Abrahamic covenant at Gen 17:8, also with olam ("everlasting"). The same phrase governs land grants in Levitical legislation (Lev 25:34). "Everlasting possession" is preferred over "perpetual possession" as more resonant in formal English and more directly echo the Genesis 17 language. The LXX renders this as κατάσχεσιν αἰώνιον (kataschesis aionion — "eternal holding"), which is in full agreement.

**Note 4 — v. 7: "some distance to go" (kibrat-ha'arets)**
This rare phrase — kibrat ha'arets — occurs only at Gen 35:16 (the original account of Rachel's death) and here. The meaning is uncertain; it is an archaic measure of distance, rendered by ancient translators variously as "a little way" (LXX: ἱκανοῦ γῆς, "a sufficient stretch of land"; cf. KJV "but a little way"). The translation "some distance" follows the consensus that it indicates a short but notable distance — not immediately at Ephrath, but close. The careful preservation of this detail in Jacob's dying words emphasizes Rachel's special place in his memory and the poignancy of her roadside burial, contrasted with the cave-tomb of Machpelah that awaits Jacob himself.

**Note 5 — v. 14: "he crossed his hands deliberately" (sikkel et-yadav)**
The verb sikkel (piel of s-k-l) is the crux of the passage. The root means "to be prudent, to act wisely, to have insight" (cf. 1 Sam 18:14; Prov 17:8). In the piel it conveys deliberate, purposeful action — not accident or absent-mindedness. The narrator is explicit: Jacob's crossing of his hands was intentional, an act of informed will overriding the conventional expectation of primogeniture. "Crossed his hands deliberately" renders both the physical act and its intentional character. The LXX uses ἐναλλάξ (enallax — "transposing, crosswise"), which captures the physical gesture but loses the element of deliberate wisdom embedded in the Hebrew verb.

**Note 6 — v. 15-16: The threefold blessing — structure and theology**
The blessing of vv. 15-16 is structured as a single sentence with three relative clauses expanding the subject "God/the angel" before the main verb "may he bless." The three designations are: (1) "the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked" — the God of covenant history; (2) "the God who has been my shepherd all my life long" — the God of personal providential care; (3) "the angel who has redeemed me from all evil" — the divine Messenger as go'el. The term go'el (redeemer) is the kinsman-redeemer of Israelite law, the one who restores what was lost (Ruth 3-4; Lev 25). Jacob applies this redemptive role to "the angel" — the same figure who wrestled with him at Penuel (32:24-30) and whom he later identified as God (32:30). The oscillation between "God" and "the angel" in this blessing is significant: early Jewish and Christian interpretation identified this angel as the pre-incarnate divine presence.

**Note 7 — v. 16: "let my name be carried on in them" (wayyiqare' bahem shemi)**
Literally "let my name be called in them" — the idiom for having one's name perpetuated through descendants. The naming of the boys as tribes of Israel rather than of Joseph fulfills this verbally: they will be called Israel's sons (v. 5). The added phrase "and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac" ties the adoption into the full chain of patriarchal covenant identity. The translation "carried on" renders the perpetuation idiom in natural English while maintaining the formal register.

**Note 8 — v. 19: "I know, my son, I know" (yada'ti beni yada'ti)**
The doubled yada'ti is emphatic: "I know — indeed, my son, I know." Jacob's double affirmation preempts any further argument from Joseph. He is not confused or forgetful; his action is fully cognizant of the birth order. The doubling of the verb is an intensive marker of certainty, paralleled by the double divine calls ("Abraham, Abraham," Gen 22:11; "Jacob, Jacob," 46:2). It is a moment of paternal authority asserted in the face of filial deference. The translation preserves the double "I know" without smoothing it into a single assertion.

**Note 9 — v. 19: "a fullness of nations" (melo' ha-goyim)**
The phrase melo' ha-goyim is unique to this verse in the Hebrew Bible. Melo' means "fullness, abundance" (cf. Ps 24:1: "the earth and its fullness"). Ha-goyim is "the nations, the peoples." Together: "the fullness of nations" — a phrase suggesting that Ephraim's offspring will constitute a plurality, an abundance of peoples. Paul alludes to this phrase in Rom 11:25 (to pleroma ton ethnon — "the fullness of the Gentiles"), the only other occurrence of this precise combination in ancient literature, suggesting Paul had Gen 48:19 in view as a scriptural subtext for the Gentile mission.

**Note 10 — v. 20: "May God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh"**
The blessing formula given here becomes the standard parental blessing for sons in Israel, still recited on Friday evenings in Jewish liturgy (Seder ha-berakha). The order "Ephraim and Manasseh" — the younger first — encodes the crossing of hands permanently into the blessing formula. To bless a son "as Ephraim and Manasseh" is to invoke a precedent in which the younger surpassed the elder — a recurrent pattern in Genesis (Abel over Cain, Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over his brothers, Ephraim over Manasseh).

**Note 11 — v. 22: "one portion" / shechem echad — the wordplay**
The Hebrew shechem carries two meanings simultaneously: (1) "shoulder, ridge, slope" — a geographical term indicating a ridge or portion of land; and (2) Shechem — the place name of the city in central Canaan. Jacob had purchased a plot of land at Shechem in Gen 33:19 and had conflict with the Shechemites in ch. 34. The phrase shechem echad ("one shechem") thus means both "one mountain portion" and alludes to the specific territory of Shechem, where Joseph's bones will eventually be buried (Josh 24:32). Both meanings coexist in the original, and no English translation can fully capture both. "One portion" renders the primary meaning while the note preserves the wordplay.

**Note 12 — v. 16: "redeemed me from all evil" (ha-mal'akh ha-go'el oti mi-kol-ra')**
The verb ga'al (to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer) is applied here not to a human go'el but to "the angel." This is the only place in the Pentateuch where the go'el role is attributed to a divine being. The phrase "from all evil" (mi-kol-ra') echoes the protective formula of the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26) and anticipates the Shepherd Psalm's "I will fear no evil" (Ps 23:4). Jacob's life — marked by deception, exile, labour, grief, and conflict — is here retroactively reframed: all of it has been a story of divine redemption-from-evil, not merely survival.

---

## LXX (Vaticanus) Variants

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus / Codex B) | Significance |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| v. 1 | "he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim" | LXX: "he took his two sons with him" — same order | No substantive variant; LXX agrees |
| v. 2 | "Israel summoned his strength and sat up on the bed" | LXX: "Israel strengthened himself and sat upon the bed" | ἐνίσχυσεν (enikhysen) = "was strengthened"; same meaning |
| v. 4 | "a company of peoples" (qahal ammim) | LXX: "gatherings of nations" (συναγωγὰς ἐθνῶν, synagogas ethnon) | LXX uses ethnon (nations/Gentiles) where MT has ammim (peoples); slight universalizing tendency |
| v. 5 | "Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine, as Reuben and Simeon are mine" | LXX: same, in same order (Ephraim before Manasseh) | LXX agrees with MT; Ephraim already placed first |
| v. 7 | "kibrat ha'arets" — "some distance to go" | LXX: ἱκανοῦ γῆς (hikanou ges) — "a sufficient stretch of land / a good way" | LXX implies a longer distance than the "little way" of some English versions |
| v. 14 | "he crossed his hands deliberately" (sikkel et-yadav) | LXX: ἐναλλάξ (enallax) — "crosswise, transposing" | LXX captures the physical act but drops the semantic component of deliberate wisdom (s-k-l) |
| v. 15 | "the God who has been my shepherd all my life long" | LXX: "the God who feeds me from my youth" (ὁ τρέφων με) | LXX uses trepho ("to nourish, feed") rather than a shepherding verb; slightly different metaphor |
| v. 16 | "the angel who has redeemed me" (ha-mal'akh ha-go'el) | LXX: ὁ ἄγγελος ὁ ῥυόμενός με (ho angelos ho ryomenos me) — "the angel who delivers/rescues me" | LXX uses rhyomai ("to rescue, deliver") rather than a redemption/kinship term; the go'el legal nuance is lost |
| v. 16 | "let them grow into a multitude" (yidgu la-rov) | LXX: "let them be multiplied to a great multitude" (πληθυνθείησαν) | LXX expands slightly; yidgu is from dag ("fish") — "may they multiply like fish" — a fishy idiom unique to this verse |
| v. 19 | "a fullness of nations" (melo' ha-goyim) | LXX: πλῆθος ἐθνῶν (plethos ethnon) — "a multitude of nations" | LXX renders melo' as plethos; loses the "fullness" connotation that Paul appears to cite in Rom 11:25 |
| v. 20 | "May God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh" | LXX: agrees in content; Ephraim before Manasseh | LXX preserves the inverted birth-order blessing formula |
| v. 22 | shechem echad — "one portion / one shechem" | LXX: μερίδα μίαν (merida mian) — "one portion" | LXX uses merida ("portion, share"), losing the Shechem place-name wordplay entirely |

---

## Structural and Theological Notes

**The Deathbed Adoption — Legal Form and Covenant Significance**

The adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh in vv. 5-6 employs formal legal language recognizable from ancient Near Eastern adoption texts. The phrase "they are mine" (li hem) is the declaratory formula by which legal adoption was effected. By adopting Joseph's two sons as his own, Jacob accomplishes several things simultaneously: (1) he doubles Joseph's inheritance share (two portions instead of one, fulfilling the firstborn's double portion even though Joseph was not the firstborn); (2) he constitutes the twelve-tribe structure that would otherwise include a "Joseph tribe" — instead there is an "Ephraim tribe" and a "Manasseh tribe," and the Levites receive no territorial allotment, preserving the number twelve; (3) he ensures that the sons born on Egyptian soil — in the land of exile — are nonetheless fully incorporated into the covenant people of Canaan. Egypt cannot absorb these sons; Israel has claimed them.

The clause in v. 6 — "they shall be called by the name of their brothers in their inheritance" — means that any further sons of Joseph would be absorbed into the Ephraim or Manasseh tribal allocations, subordinate to these two. This is a permanent, legally binding act.

**The Crossing of Hands — Genesis's Recurring Reversal**

The crossing of hands in v. 14 is the climactic gesture of a book filled with primogeniture reversals. The pattern runs from the beginning: Abel preferred over Cain; Isaac over Ishmael; Jacob over Esau (with the birthright and the blessing obtained by deliberate deception); Judah and Joseph elevated over their older brothers; Perez over Zerah (with the scarlet thread, Gen 38:27-30); and now Ephraim over Manasseh. The verb sikkel ("to act wisely/deliberately") is the narrator's theological commentary: this is not error or senility. Jacob has divine insight. He sees what Joseph cannot see — that the younger will exceed the elder. This is the grammar of grace throughout Genesis: God's election does not follow human expectation.

The historical fulfillment is visible throughout Israel's later history. Ephraim becomes the dominant tribe of the northern kingdom — so dominant that "Ephraim" becomes a synecdoche for the entire northern nation (Hos 4:17; Isa 7:2; 11:13). The blessing of the "younger" tribe shapes the entire subsequent history of the twelve.

**Jacob's Threefold Divine Description — A Christological Locus**

The threefold description of God in vv. 15-16 — as covenant God, as shepherd, and as redeeming angel — has attracted intensive theological attention throughout the history of interpretation. The juxtaposition of "God" (ha-Elohim, twice) and "the angel" (ha-mal'akh) in a single sentence, with a single verb ("may he bless"), strongly implies either identity or the most intimate participation between the two. The Targums consistently identify "the angel" with the divine Memra (Word). Early Christian commentators (Justin Martyr, Origen, Augustine) identified this angel as the pre-incarnate Son. Whether or not that reading is pressed, the passage is remarkable in ascribing the go'el role — the legal-relational role of kinship redemption — to a divine being. Jacob's life of exile, deception, loss, and unexpected grace has taught him that God himself functions as his nearest kinsman, obligated by covenant-love to buy him back from every form of bondage.

The shepherd image (v. 15: "who has shepherded me all my life long") is the only use of this metaphor for God's care of an individual patriarch in Genesis. It stands behind Ps 23 and the whole shepherd theology of the Hebrew Bible (Ezek 34; Isa 40:11; John 10).

**Rachel's Death and the Geography of Grief**

The parenthetical recollection of Rachel's death in v. 7 — inserted between the adoption declaration and its confirmation — is not a textual intrusion but a deliberate authorial placement. Jacob adopts the Egyptian-born sons of Joseph partly because Joseph is the son of Rachel, the wife he loved supremely. Rachel died outside Canaan, without reaching the land of promise. Her burial place (not the family tomb of Machpelah but a roadside marker at Ephrath) embodies her incompleteness and her perpetual "outsideness." By adopting her grandsons as full tribal founders of Israel, Jacob posthumously incorporates Rachel's line fully and irrevocably into the covenant inheritance she never lived to possess. It is a gesture of grief-informed faithfulness: the patriarch rectifies, through blessing, what death interrupted.

**The "Fullness of Nations" and Pauline Hermeneutics**

The phrase melo' ha-goyim in v. 19 is without parallel elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible. Paul's phrase to pleroma ton ethnon in Rom 11:25 ("the fullness of the Gentiles") appears to echo it directly. If so, Paul reads the coming-in of Gentiles into the covenant people as the fulfillment of Jacob's blessing on Ephraim — the "nations" that would flow through Ephraim's line become the Gentile multitude that "comes in" before the eschatological restoration of Israel. This is not an allegorization but a typological reading grounded in the lexical rarity of the phrase: Paul draws on the only prior occurrence of "fullness of nations" in all of Israel's scripture.
